1: coniunx: Nowhere else in the Amores does a speaker refer to his amatory interest as a coniunx, and apart from Amores 3.5.16, wherein the word refers to an animal’s mate (cf. OLD s.v. coniunx 2), the sense is routinely one of marriage or permanent partnership. For comparison, consider the differences between English “partner” or “spouse” and “girlfriend.” Because of this word, editors have tended to read the poem autobiographically, as if Ovid is speaking about a festival in his wife’s hometown. Tristia 4.10 is an autobiographical poem in which the speaker far more closely identified with the historical Ovid refers to a wife and two previous marriages, repeatedly using the word coniunx (69-74) alongside uxor. In any case, the mention of a coniunx here in Amores 3.13 might reasonably jar readers out of the norms of amatory elegy and prepare them for something different, in this case, an aetiological poem about a festival for Juno Curitis, the patron goddess of the Falerians (Falisci), a city north of Rome in southern Etruria. pomiferis…Faliscis: the speaker does not specify in what manner Falerii was “fruitful” (pomiferi), but the region is now known for its olive and wine production along with its agrotourism.
2: Camille: Marcus Furius Camillus was a Roman military figure integral to the capture of Veii in 396 BCE. Plutarch’s Life of Camillus (10) recounts his “conquest” of Falerii after a lengthy siege that failed to penetrate the city’s walls. The war was won not through military might but, as Plutarch asserts, through Roman virtue. When a Falerian teacher surrendered his (Falerian) students to Camillus as an act of treachery, Camillus, then military tribune, bound the teacher and gave the students weapons with which to drive him back to the city. According to Plutarch, Camillus’s sense of justice (δικαιοσύνη) persuaded the Falerians to surrender. tibi: a dative of agent with uicta.
3: sacerdotes: the title is gender neutral, but Juno was attended by priestesses.
4: indigenam: Falerii was known for its snow-white (see v. 13) local cattle.
5: morae pretium: “a return for time spent” (OLD s.v. pretium 2) The sense, with grande, is that the trip to see the festival is “well worth the delay.” cognoscere: a subject infinitive with est implied.
6: cliuis: the ablative explains in what way the uia is difficilis. Falerii is identified with modern-day Civita Castellana. To get a sense of the hilliness of the journey, you can consult an elevation profile for the ~65 km journey by mapping the route for a bicycle trip (e.g., on Google Maps).
7: stat uetus et: the same three words begin Amores 3.1.1. praenubilus: a “hapax legomenon” (ἅπαξ λεγόμενον = “being said once”). The term designates a word that is used only once in a body of literature.
8: aspice, concedes: addressing a singular addressee, the speaker invites the individual reader to imagine the space described. The line echoes the sense of 3.1.2; this grove, like that described in the opening of 3.1, is a numinous place.
10: sine arte: the simplicity of the altar testifies to its antiquity.
11: huc: the speaker begins his description of the procession with route’s destination.
12: uelatas: the parade route may be shaded with foliage or otherwise decorated (OLD s.v. velō 3b), as bunting decorates modern parade routes.
14: herba Falisca: did I read somewhere that they thought the local feed led to the coloring of the heifers?
15: nondum: the adverb neutralizes metuenda which itself modifies fronte. fronte minaces: The calves “threaten” with undeveloped horns (OLD s.v. frons 1d).
16: uictima: a nominative noun in apposition with minor…porcus.
17: cornu…recuruo: although the mention of a flock (gregis) could apply to cattle, sheep, or goats, the emphasis on curling horns and hard temples (tempora dura) suggests a ram. The procession of sacrificial victims bears some resemblance to a suovetaurilia, or a sacred sacrifice of a pig (sus), sheep (ovis), and a bull (taurus).
18: capella: the she-goat is singled out for the goddess’s hatred, and it is not initially clear whether she participates in the procession. As 21-22 indicate, however, she appears to begin the procession but not make it to its conclusion.
19: illius indicio: the speaker offers an aetiology (one particularly thin on details) for the goddess’s animus toward the she-goat. Juno, presumably hiding, was discovered “on the evidence of that one” (i.e., the she-goat) and aborted an attempted flight. The text provides no further details, but Green 1982: 335 explains that “Hera’s flight from Zeus is presumably to be related to the period before their marriage, when brother and sister became involved without their parents’ knowledge […].” The manuscript Kenney designates “C” (but De Verger designates “Ed” in his revised Teubner edition) offers further explanation in a marginal note: https://digital.nls.uk/early-manuscripts/browse/archive/234773632#?c=0&m=0&s=0&cv=125&xywh=6036%2C1553%2C1687%2C993
21: per pueros iaculis: the boys by whom the informer is attacked (cf. per with the accusative in A&G 405b) are distinguished from their means of attack. The practice resembles a scapegoat ritual, but the practicalities are unclear. Would the boys throw lethal projectiles at the goat during the procession? Might the language be dramatizing some sort of game, like a carnival ring toss? For non-lethal iacula of flower blossoms, see Apuleius Met. 10.32.
22: pretium: revealed to be in apposition with ipsa by the end of the verse. The she-goat is awarded to whoever authors her wound. Is the she-goat symbolically wounded? Genuinely wounded? Killed? Bremmer 1983: 315-318 summarizes the evidence for the death of the scapegoat (vs. expulsion) in Greek rituals.
23: qua: “to where” uentura…est: the active periphrastic gives a sense of the imminence of the goddess’s arrival.
24: praeuerrunt: a “dis legomenon,” meaning this prefixed form of uerrō appears only twice in extant Latin. Yet it does not appear in Y, the only of our oldest manuscripts with a text of Amores 3.13; Y reads praebuerat. Kenney’s apparatus criticus indicates that praeuerrunt appears as a variant reading in Manuscript C, a 15th c. manuscript now in the National Library of Scotland collections, and in excerpta Puteani et Scaligeri, the latter of which are marginal readings in a copy of Ovid’s works by the Franco-Italian scholar Joseph Justus Scaliger (1540-1609). Manuscript C v.l. ueste iacente: the trailing garments of the youth sweep the procession route clean in preparation for Juno.
25: In this and the next couplet, the speaker details accessories and clothing worn by the girls (puellae, 23): golden, gemmed tiaras and gilt shoes (nearly) covered by majestic robes.
27: more patrum Graio: the first indication that the ritual has a Greek pedigree. uelatae: the puellae (23) remain the implied subject.
28: tradita: the participle may emphasize the traditional nature (OLD s.v. trādō 4) of the sacred objects (sacra). supposito uertice: the means by which the puellae carry the sacra.
29: ore fauent: the formulation implies reverent silence. Cf. fauete linguis in Cic. Div. 2.83 and Hor. Carm. 3.1.
30: ipsa: the cult statue of Juno herself. suas: Manuscript Y contains suos, but several other later texts contain suas.
31: Argiua: the speaker expands upon the Greek features of the procession. “Argive” relates to Argos, an ancient city-state on the Greek Peloponnese which asserted a special relationship with the goddess Hera (Iliad 4.50). The remains of an ancient sanctuary to Hera still survive near the ancient city. facies: the “characteristic appearance,” “form,” or “manner” (OLD s.v. facies 4). At a stretch, one might be tempted to translate this as “origin,” as it sets up another aetiological explanation. Agamemnone caeso: Agamenon, the leader of the Greek forces in the Trojan War, was murdered by his wife Klytemnestra upon his return to Argos after the war. The Oresteia, a trilogy of plays by the Attic tragedian Aeschylus, offers the most famous version of the murder and its aftermath.
32: Halaesus: Orestes is the famous son of Agamemnon and Klytemnestra, but Halaesus may have been a son of Agamemnon born outside of their marriage. Vergil gives this man the epithet Agamemnonius in Aeneid 7.723, suggesting a paternal relationship.
33: pererratis…terraque fretoque: the plural ablative participle modifies the singular nouns emphatically joined in polysyndeton.
35: ille: Halaesus is designated as the “teacher” of the rites observed throughout the poem.
36: sint: the subject of this verb (and the following sint) is supplied by Iunonia sacra in the previous verse.
